card.title: The 4:1 Rule for Safe Ladder Use
card.teaser: One quick ratio keeps your ladder from turning into a catapult. Learn it before you climb one again.
card.coverImagePrompt: An extension ladder leaning against a sunlit suburban house at a careful angle, fall leaves scattered at the base, long afternoon shadows, photorealistic warm tones

lessons.title: Why ladders fall
lessons.body: Every year about 500,000 Americans land in the ER after a ladder accident. Most weren't doing anything reckless — they set the ladder too steep and it flipped backward, or too shallow and the feet shot out from under them. There's a shockingly simple ratio that prevents nearly all of this. It's been in safety codes for over a century.

visual.imagePrompt: A conceptual illustration showing two bad ladder angles (too vertical, too shallow) versus one correct angle, stick-figure style

lessons.title: The 4-to-1 rule
lessons.body: For every 4 feet of ladder height you climb, the base should be 1 foot away from the wall. A ladder reaching a 12-foot gutter? Its feet go 3 feet out. Reaching 20 feet? 5 feet out. That ratio puts the ladder at roughly 75 degrees from the ground — the angle where your weight pushes straight down the rails instead of tipping you forward or sliding the feet out.

keyTerms.definition: For every 4 feet of vertical reach, place the ladder base 1 foot away from the wall
The ideal angle the ladder makes with the ground — a direct result of the 4:1 geometry

visual.imagePrompt: Annotated geometric diagram: a ladder at a 75-degree angle with the 4:1 ratio labeled, wall on the left, ground on the bottom

lessons.title: The body-check shortcut
lessons.body: No measuring tape? There's a trick firefighters use: stand with your toes touching the ladder's feet, arms stretched straight out. If the ladder is at the correct angle, your palms will just barely touch a rung at shoulder height. If your arms are bent, it's too steep. If you can't reach, it's too shallow. Takes three seconds and works every time.
lessons.funFact: The 4:1 angle also happens to be the most efficient for climbing — less energy spent fighting the angle, more spent actually moving up.

visual.imagePrompt: A homeowner standing at the base of a ladder, arms stretched straight out touching a rung, demonstrating the body-check method

lessons.title: Three extra rules worth knowing
lessons.body: Once your angle is set, remember three more: (1) the ladder should extend at least 3 feet above any landing surface like a roof edge, (2) maintain three points of contact while climbing — two hands and a foot, or two feet and a hand, and (3) never stand on the top two rungs. Combined with 4:1, these rules eliminate the overwhelming majority of ladder accidents. OSHA has built entire construction safety programs around them.

quiz.question: Using the 4:1 rule, where should the base go for a ladder reaching a 16-foot gutter?
quiz.explanation: 16 divided by 4 equals 4 — so place the base 4 feet away from the wall.

quiz.question: What does the 4:1 rule approximately correspond to in angle terms?
quiz.explanation: A 4:1 height-to-base ratio creates an angle of roughly 75 degrees from the ground.

quiz.question: What does 'three points of contact' mean while climbing a ladder?
options: Two hands and a foot, or two feet and a hand
quiz.explanation: Maintain two hands and a foot, or two feet and a hand on the ladder at all times — this prevents falls if one slips.

sources.title: Stairways and Ladders: A Guide to OSHA Rules
sources.publisher: U.S. Occupational Safety and Health Administration

sources.title: Ladder-Related Injuries Treated in U.S. Emergency Departments
